schools.summary: A second, much later visit — 'fourteen years after' (reckoned by most from the first), marking long years of independent labour; with Barnabas (fellow-worker) and Titus (an uncircumcised Greek, a living test case); many identify this with the Jerusalem council (Acts 15), though Calvin demurs.

sides.position: the Jerusalem council of Acts 15, where Gentile circumcision was settled
an earlier visit, not the council

schools.summary: He went 'by revelation' (divine direction, harmonizing with Acts 15:2's record that the church sent him); 'communicated' his gospel not to be confirmed but to show its agreement and secure unity; 'privately to them of reputation' (discreet, avoiding needless controversy); 'lest I should run in vain' — concern not for his gospel but lest a breach frustrate his labours.

Titus, a Greek brought as a test case, 'was not compelled to be circumcised' — the Jerusalem leaders receiving an uncircumcised man as a brother; settling the principle that circumcision is not required for justification or membership (Calvin); the very outcome revealing the conference's conclusion (Luther).

'False brethren' (pseudadelphous, professing Christ but enemies of grace) 'unawares brought in' (smuggled in by stealth, like spies) to 'spy out our liberty in Christ' (freedom from the ceremonial yoke) and 'bring us into bondage'; these, not the apostles, pressed for Titus's circumcision, the leaders' refusal a stand against them.

'No, not for an hour' did they yield in subjection to the false brethren's demand; Paul not rigid for its own sake (he circumcised Timothy for weak brethren) but refused Titus because here it was demanded as necessary to salvation; 'that the truth of the gospel might continue with you' — concession would have betrayed free justification; the steadfastness sealing his doctrine (Calvin).

themes: firm where truth is at stake

schools.summary: 'Who seemed to be somewhat' (of acknowledged reputation — Cephas, James, John, no disrespect) 'added nothing to me' — the chief apostles found nothing to correct or supplement in his gospel; 'God accepteth no man's person' — their eminence makes their gospel no truer, his no inferior; Luther: he abases their titles to exalt the truth.

'Contrariwise' the apostles positively recognized that God entrusted Paul 'the gospel of the uncircumcision' (the Gentile mission) as Peter 'the gospel of the circumcision' (the Jewish) — one gospel, two spheres of labour, not two gospels; his teaching fundamentally identical to theirs, his success proving the divine call (Ellicott).

themes: committed unto me as to Peter

schools.summary: The same verb (energeo) in both clauses — God 'wrought effectually' in Peter and 'was mighty' in Paul, one divine energy through each; the working (conversions, signs, manifest power) the proof and seal of a true apostleship, which God would not grant a false apostle (Calvin); Paul's mission on the identical footing as Peter's.

The 'pillars' (styloi, chief supports — James, Cephas, John), perceiving 'the grace given unto me' (the divine grace in his call and fruitful labours), gave 'the right hands of fellowship' (the solemn token of partnership and mutual recognition) to Paul and Barnabas as equals; by this the pillars testified he was a true apostle (Luther).

The apostles added 'only' one request, and that not doctrinal — 'remember the poor' (the impoverished believers of Jerusalem/Judea, Acts 11:29-30), which Paul was already 'forward' to do; had they thought his gospel defective they would have added doctrine, but added only a work of charity, confirming they found his message complete.
Luther: remembrance of the poor belongs to the office of a faithful pastor.

'I withstood him to the face' — Paul openly rebuked Peter as an equal when the gospel's truth was at stake, 'because he was to be blamed' (genuinely in the wrong); the crowning proof of his standing — he could correct the foremost apostle; the great majority hold this 'Cephas' is Peter himself.
Calvin places this before the Acts 15 decree settled the matter.

Peter, knowing the wall abolished, freely 'ate with the Gentiles' (table-fellowship, as his Joppa vision taught); but when 'certain came from James' (the circumcision party) he 'withdrew and separated himself, fearing' them — not from conviction but human respect; not heresy but inconsistency, acting against his own knowledge.

The fault spread — 'the other Jews dissembled likewise' (synypekrithesan, joining the pretence), suppressing their real convictions out of fear; 'even Barnabas' (Paul's fellow-labourer, the Gentiles' champion) 'was carried away as by a torrent'; the power of a leader's bad example, the contagion running through the rest, threatening to divide the church.

Seeing they 'walked not uprightly' (orthopodousin, not straight) 'according to the truth of the gospel,' Paul rebuked Peter 'before them all' (public fault, public rebuke); the unanswerable logic — Peter, a Jew, had lived 'after the manner of Gentiles' (eating freely), so by withdrawing he wrongly 'compelled the Gentiles to live as Jews'; his own practice condemning him.

The turn from rebuke to doctrine (whether spoken to Peter or now to the Galatians) — 'Jews by nature' (born in the covenant, possessing the law) 'and not sinners of the Gentiles' (the Jewish term for the lawless heathen); Paul takes up the Judaizers' own advantage to show even this gives no ground of justification; the foundation doctrine begun (Henry).

The cardinal doctrine — 'justified' (dikaioutai, declared righteous before God's tribunal) 'not by the works of the law' (no obedience, ceremonial or moral, Rom 3:20,28) 'but by the faith of Jesus Christ'; thrice stated, 'no flesh' (Jew or Gentile) justified by law, for all have sinned — the truth that overturns the Judaizing system.
Luther: 'the works of the law' embrace everything that is not grace; Calvin: justification by the gift of Christ alone.

themes: not justified by works of the law
but by faith of Jesus Christ

schools.summary: An objection answered — if Jews, seeking justification in Christ, are found 'sinners' like the Gentiles (outside the law), is Christ thereby 'the minister of sin' (a promoter of sinful living)? 'God forbid'; those who come to Christ do discover they are sinners (self-righteousness stripped away), but this is the first step to grace, not Christ's fault.

themes: is Christ a minister of sin?

schools.summary: The fault located — to 'build again' the law as a ground of justification, after 'destroying' it (preaching its insufficiency and pointing to Christ), is to prove oneself 'a transgressor' (parabaten); Peter, by withdrawing, rebuilt the wall he had torn down; the sin is in going back from Christ to the law, not in leaving it (Ellicott).

themes: the fault is returning to the law

schools.summary: The paradox 'through the law am dead to the law' (Rom 7:4) — the law itself, by its demands and curse, slew every hope of justification by works and drove him to Christ; the law slays its own disciples (Calvin); the liberating purpose 'that I might live unto God' — death to the law the means to a new life directed wholly to God, not lawlessness.

themes: through the law dead to the law
that I might live unto God
death as the means to life

schools.summary: Union with Christ — 'crucified with Christ' (synestauromai, perfect; the old self put to death, Rom 6:6), 'yet not I, but Christ liveth in me' (the source of the new life Christ within); 'I live by the faith of the Son of God' (sustained by trust); 'who loved me, and gave himself for me' — the atonement individualized to the single soul.

themes: live by the faith of the Son of God
'who loved me, and gave himself for me'

schools.summary: The unanswerable conclusion — 'I do not frustrate the grace of God' (atheto, make void; the charge belongs to the Judaizers who nullify grace by adding law); 'if righteousness come by the law, then Christ is dead in vain' — had the law sufficed, Christ need not have died; the cross the standing proof the law cannot justify; grace and law mutually exclusive as grounds of righteousness.
Luther: to seek justification by works is to reject the grace of God; Calvin: dreadful ingratitude, despising grace bought at such a price.

themes: frustrate not the grace of God
